TextENGNew: The cicadas' chorus grew ever more lively.
That eternal June had brought freakish weather -- heat that made it feel like summer had already arrived, despite it only being June.
But even so, June was still just June.
Now that July had drawn us even closer to true summer, the days felt more and more like the real thing.
Today, we all went to the swimming pool together -- a dream of mine finally fulfilled.
It might seem like a modest dream at a glance, but there was no need to look back anymore on the hundred-year journey it took to reach it.
Still, I felt it was something I should never forget.
For most people, this was just an ordinary day -- but for me, it had taken a dizzying number of wandering days just to arrive here.
Precisely because I remembered that, I could feel such deep happiness in these seemingly plain, unremarkable days.
``Ha ha ha ha!``@k@vS15/03/DS42030002. Not bringing a camera today may be Mion Sonozaki's greatest blunder in life!``

``Hau, Rena doesn't need a camera~.``@k@vS15/02/DS42020002. I've burned it all right into my memory.``@k@|@y@vS15/02/DS42020003. When I close my eyes, I can still see how adorable Keiichi-kun looked~. Hau~☆``
changeReason: New translation preserves Rena's characteristic third-person self-reference ('Rena doesn't need') matching the Japanese, whereas old used first person ('I don't need'). Also adds '-kun' honorific to Keiichi's name.

TextENGNew: ``Gah!!@k @vS15/01/DS42010002. Don't picture it! Stop picturing it!@k @vS15/01/DS42010003. I'll sand those eyelids right off your face!!``
changeReason: Old translates 浮かばんでいい as 'Don't look at them' (visual) while JPN means 'Don't picture it' (mental imagery); new correctly captures the mental visualization.

TextENGNew: ``Going to the pool and forgetting your swimsuit -- that's entirely your own fault.``@k@vS15/04/DS42040002. And the whole commotion over those dubious swim trunks -- from start to finish, you brought it all on yourself!``

``...Keiichi, you should be grateful to Mii.@k@vS15/05/DS42050002. If she hadn't brought those swan underpants, you'd have been buck naked and it would have been quite the disaster.@k@vS15/05/DS42050003. Nipah~☆``
changeReason: Old adds innuendo ('your swan would have been swinging all over') not present in JPN すっぽんぽんで大変なことに (simply 'buck naked and it would have been quite the disaster').

TextENGNew: ``Heh heh heh!``@k@vS15/03/DS42030004. Boy, that was truly a sight to behold.``@k@|@y@vS15/03/DS42030005. I hadn't planned anything specific for the pool today, and was thinking we'd just have a nice, quiet time splashing around... heh heh heh!``
``With this crew, it'd be stranger if nothing happened!``
``No way, that's absolutely not true!``

``If you hadn't planned anything, there'd be no reason to bring those embarrassing swan underpants in the first place!!``@k@vS15/01/DS42010006. You were definitely scheming to find some excuse to make me wear them!!``
``Ahahahaha, but it was such a fun day! Rena is completely satisfied! She is!``
changeReason: New translation preserves Rena's third-person self-reference ('Rena is completely satisfied! She is!') matching the Japanese, whereas old used first person ('I'm totally satisfied! I am!').

TextENGNew: ``Indeed!``@k@vS15/04/DS42040004. On that point, I wholeheartedly agree!``
Satoko turned to me with a smile, seeking my agreement.
So I returned a smile just as bright, showing her how much fun the day's chaos had been.
As we all looked back on the day's pool antics, we hopped on our bikes and headed home.
There were no pools in Hinamizawa, so we'd gone all the way to Okinomiya, which had a public one.
There was a significant difference in elevation between the two towns, so the ride to Okinomiya was a pleasant downhill coast, but the return trip meant a long climb.
For energetic Hinamizawa children, it wasn't much of a hardship, but even so, the long incline naturally made us fall quiet as we pedaled away in silence.
Seeing everyone go so quiet made me itch to stir up some mischief.
Poking at Satoko at times like these was so much fun for me that you could practically call it my life's work.
I pedaled harder, caught up to Satoko's bike as she rode along in silence, and gave her shoulder a playful shove.
``Whoa-ack!``@k@vS15/04/DS42040006. R-Rikaaaa!!``

changeReason: New translation uses 'Mii!' as a direct transliteration of Rika's characteristic みー vocalization, replacing the localized 'Meep!'

TextENGNew: After making a teasing face at Satoko, I picked up speed.
Naturally, Satoko sped up too and chased after me.
She tried her hardest to nudge me back, but I wasn't about to let her catch me that easily.
Our speed picked up naturally, and before long we'd blown right past the rest of the group.
``Ooh, a race~?!``@k@vS15/03/DS42030008. This ol' man won't lose either!``
``Don't fool around on the road!``@k@vS15/02/DS42020006. Rika-chan, Satoko-chan, it's dangerous!``
This was a public road, so cars did pass through from time to time.
But having lived here for so long, we knew hardly any cars came at this hour.
``Rika!``@k@vS15/04/DS42040008. There's a car coming!!``
``...Mii~!``@k@vS15/05/DS42050006. I'm not falling for that trick!``
changeReason: New translation uses 'Mii~!' as a direct transliteration of Rika's みー vocalization, replacing the localized 'Meep~!'

TextENGNew: So when Satoko called out, I assumed it was just a ploy to distract me.
A car horn.
Hearing it, I realized Satoko's warning was no trick.
...But it was so sudden that shock and confusion took hold of me.
When I looked ahead, the car was right there.
I panicked and wrenched my handlebars to the side.
The motion was so violent and abrupt that it was more than enough to throw me off balance.
I felt my body pitch forward and leave the ground.
...The acrid smell that comes just before pain hit my nostrils.
changeReason: Old translation says 'whiff of asphalt' but the Japanese 火薬の臭い means 'smell of gunpowder'; new translation renders it as 'the acrid smell that comes just before pain,' avoiding the factual error.

TextENGNew: ``Rikaaaaaaa!!!``
I heard Satoko scream.
...No -- "thought I heard" was more accurate. That was how unreal it felt.
I couldn't even comprehend whether the sight of the car bearing down on me as I tumbled through the air was actually happening.
But that absence of reality probably lasted only a single second.
I wasn't even given time to form the word "ah."
changeReason: Old translation renders あ as 'oops' (implying mistake realization) while the Japanese simply means 'ah'; new translation 'form the word ah' is more accurate to the reflexive gasp before impact.
